---
title: "4.4 Partial Traversals and Common Errors"
subtitle: "Array Traversals"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Looping through Part of an Array

### Not always the whole array

You do not have to loop through all of the elements of an array.

You can loop through just some of the elements of an array using a `for` loop.

The source code doubles the first five elements in an array.

It uses a complex conditional with `&&` to make sure the loop does not go beyond the length of the array.

## Why the Extra Condition

### Avoid a missing element

If you had an array with fewer than 5 elements, you would not want the code to try to double the 5th element, which does not exist.

In this code, the array is a private instance variable of the class `ArrayWorker`.

It is created in the constructor and changed or accessed by the methods.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: tripleFirstFour`

What will the following code print out?

Can you write a similar method called `tripleFirstFour()` that triples the first four elements of the array?

Make sure you test it in `main`.

## tripleFirstFour Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class ArrayWorker
{
    private int[] values;

    public ArrayWorker(int[] theValues)
    {
        values = theValues;
    }

    /** Doubles the first 5 elements of the array */
    public void doubleFirstFive()
    {
        // Notice: && i < 5
        for (int i = 0; i < values.length && i < 5; i++)
        {
            values[i] = values[i] * 2;
        }
    }

    /**
     * Write a method called tripleFirstFour() that triples
     * the first 4 elements of the array *
     */
    public void tripleFirstFour()
    {
        // TODO: Triple the first four elements without going past values.length

    }

    /** Prints the array */
    public void printArray()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < values.length; i++)
        {
            System.out.println(values[i]);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] numArray = {3, 8, -3, 2, 20, 5, 33, 1};
        ArrayWorker worker = new ArrayWorker(numArray);
        worker.doubleFirstFive();
        worker.printArray();
        // TODO: uncomment these to test
        // worker.tripleFirstFour();
        // worker.printArray();
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Targets

### `doubleFirstFive`

The first expected output after `doubleFirstFive()` is:

```text
6
16
-6
4
40
5
33
1
```

## Test Targets

### `tripleFirstFour`

The `tripleFirstFour()` test uses array `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` and expects:

```text
0
3
6
9
4
5
```

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq_array_doublelast`

Given:

```java
private int[] a = {-20, -15, 2, 8, 16, 33};

public void doubleLast()
{
    for (int i = a.length / 2; i < a.length; i++)
    {
        a[i] = a[i] * 2;
    }
}
```

What will the values of `a` be after `doubleLast()`?

## Answer Reasoning

### Correct answer

Correct array:

```text
{-20, -15, 2, 16, 32, 66}
```

It loops from the middle to the end, doubling each value.

Since there are six elements, it starts at index `3`.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq_array_mystery`

Given:

```java
private int[] a = {-20, -15, 2, 8, 16, 33};

public void mystery()
{
    for (int i = 0; i < a.length / 2; i += 2)
    {
        a[i] = a[i] * 2;
    }
}
```

What will the values of `a` be after `mystery()`?

## Answer Reasoning

### Correct answer

Correct array:

```text
{-40, -15, 4, 8, 16, 33}
```

This loops from the beginning to the middle and doubles every other element.

`i += 2` is the same as `i = i + 2`.

## Common Errors

### Off-by-one errors

When processing all array elements, start at the first index, `0`, and end at the last index.

Usually loops are written so that the index starts at `0` and continues while the index is less than `arrayName.length`.

Since `arrayName.length - 1` is the index for the last element, make sure you do not use `<=` instead of `<`.

## ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

### Index outside valid range

If the index is less than `0` or greater than `arrayName.length - 1`, an **ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException** will be thrown.

**Off by one** errors, where you go off the array by one element, are easy to make when traversing an array.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qIndexOutOfBounds`

Which loop headers will cause an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` while traversing `scores`?

::: {.tight-list}
- `for (int i = 0; i < scores.length; i++)`
- `for (int i = 1; i < scores.length; i++)`
- `for (int i = 0; i <= scores.length; i++)`
- `for (int i = 0; scores.length > i; i++)`
- `for (int i = scores.length - 1; i >= 0; i++)`
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### Two unsafe headers

Unsafe:

::: {.tight-list}
- `i <= scores.length`, because `scores.length` is one past the last valid index
- `i = scores.length - 1; i >= 0; i++`, because `i++` keeps incrementing past the end
:::

The last header should use `i--`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: offbyone`

The following code has an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.

It has two common off-by-one errors in the loop.

Can you fix it and make the loop print out all the scores?

## offbyone Starter

```java
public class OffByone
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] scores = {10, 9, 8, 7};
        // Make this loop print out all the scores!
        // TODO: Fix both off-by-one errors in this loop
        for (int i = 1; i <= scores.length; i++)
        {
            System.out.println(scores[i]);
        }
    }
}
```

## offbyone Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The tests check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- output that includes `9`, `8`, `7`
- output that includes `10`, `9`, `8`
- a fixed loop header containing `for (int i = 0; i <`
:::

## Early Return Error

### Do not jump out too soon

Be careful not to jump out of a loop too early when you are looking for a value in an array.

The method below uses `return` statements to stop the execution of the method and return a value.

However, you must be careful not to stop the loop too soon.

## findString Starter

### `activecode:: find_loop_early_return_error`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class StringWorker
{
    private String[] arr = {"Hello", "Hey", "Good morning!"};

    public int findString(String target)
    {
        String word = null;
        for (int index = 0; index < arr.length; index++)
        {
            word = arr[index];

            if (word.equals(target))
            {
                return index;
            }
            else
            {
                // TODO: Do not return -1 before checking all elements
                return -1;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        StringWorker sWorker = new StringWorker();
        System.out.println(sWorker.findString("Hey"));
    }
}
```
:::

## findString Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The corrected method should:

::: {.tight-list}
- print `1` from `main`
- return `2` for `findString("Good morning!")`
- return `-1` for `findString("Hello!")`
:::

The fix is to return `-1` only after the loop has processed all array elements.

## Infinite Loop Check

### `mchoice:: mcq_array_infinite`

Assume `temp` is an `int` variable initialized to a value greater than `0`, and `a` is an array of integers.

```java
for (int k = 0; k < a.length; k++)
{
    while (a[k] < temp)
    {
        a[k] *= 2;
    }
}
```

Which values in `a` will cause an infinite loop?

## Answer Reasoning

### Correct answer

An infinite loop occurs whenever `a` includes a value that is less than or equal to `0`.

When `a[k]` is less than or equal to `0`, multiplying by `2` will never make it larger than `temp`, which is greater than `0`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- use `&& i < limit` to traverse only part of an array safely
- trace middle-to-end and every-other-element loops
- identify `<= array.length` as an off-by-one error
- explain why a backward loop must use `i--`
- move `return -1` after a search loop
- explain the zero/negative infinite-loop case
:::

## End

### 4.4 Part 2 complete

Next: enhanced for-loops.
